ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese pho with rare beef; closest parent 'beef noodle soup food product' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Beef soup) is the closest defensible parent; no specific pho code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code found for Vietnamese pho with rare beef.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Vietnamese beef pho weighing approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, rare beef slices, bean sprouts, and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese pho with rare Australian beef exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef noodle soup (FDC ID 172934), rice noodles (FDC ID 168917), and lean beef (FDC ID 174032), weighted to reflect a typical pho composition: approximately 40% broth, 35% rice noodles, 20% beef slices, 5% garnish. Per-100g values derived from this composite. Sodium is elevated due to bone broth base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles and beef soup composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese pho with rare beef. GI estimated at 40 based on rice noodles (GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) adjusted downward for the high protein and fat content of the dish (beef slices and bone broth), which slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (40 × 36 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 14.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 1900 mg sodium, which represents 83–95% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of 14.4 per bowl is moderate; the high protein content from beef slices helps blunt postprandial glucose rise, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of rice noodles and consider requesting a smaller noodle portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef and bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~1900 mg per bowl) and saturated fat (~3.5 g per bowl) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing lean beef cuts and requesting low-sodium broth can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vietnamese Beef Pho with Rare Australian Beef (Phở bò tái bò Úc; 澳洲新鲜牛肉片越南河粉) is a Vietnamese noodle soup dish served in Singapore, consisting of flat rice noodles (bánh phở) submerged in a deeply aromatic bone broth that has been simmered for many hours with charred onion, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and fish sauce. The defining feature of this variant is thinly sliced rare Australian beef (bò tái), which is placed raw into the bowl and gently cooked by the hot broth at the moment of serving, resulting in tender, pink, silky slices with a clean beefy flavour. The dish is garnished with fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, sawtooth coriander, lime wedges, and sliced chilli, allowing diners to customise the flavour profile. The broth is rich in collagen and minerals from the bones, with a complex sweet-savoury-spiced character. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~340 kcal per bowl), significant protein (~29 g per bowl) from the beef, and moderate carbohydrate (~36 g per bowl) from the rice noodles. Sodium content is high (~1900 mg per bowl) due to the broth and fish sauce, which is a primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is moderate (estimated GL ~14), making portion control of noodles advisable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional form, though cross-contamination risk exists in restaurant settings. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Vietnamese beef pho?
What are the ingredients in pho?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish (Vietnamese pho with rare Australian beef) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for beef noodle soup, rice noodles, and lean beef. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database adjusted for macronutrient composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Trans fat is present at a trace level (0.05 g per 100 g) from naturally occurring trans fats in beef; this is not industrially produced trans fat. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Viet'Spice) does not carry a Halal certification from MUIS Singapore as of the data collection date. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu string 'P2. Rare Australian Beef 澳洲新鲜牛肉片越南河粉' to the canonical English form 'Vietnamese Beef Pho with Rare Australian Beef'; the original menu string is preserved in aliases. Cuisine is classified as Vietnamese (not Singaporean) as pho is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming follows Singaporean dish rules as this is a Singapore-context food record requiring English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi coverage.

improvement_actions_taken: Rebuilt complete RESULT JSON from scratch conforming to RESULT_template.json structure after previous iteration returned only the raw input seed record.
Normalised food_name from field survey menu string to canonical English form.
Added all mandatory local-language names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Vietnamese.
Constructed nutrient composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with full provenance.
Set glycemic_index to 40 (analogous estimate from rice noodle GI data, University of Sydney GI Database), gi_category to 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving to 14.4.
Populated ontology with FoodOn closest_parent and FoodEx2 closest_parent; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Added all three health_context conditions with clinical guidance.
Ensured all -1 sentinel values documented in notes (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg).
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
